news_summary: Ancient cultural treasures gained new recognition and accessibility with Sardis becoming a UNESCO World Heritage site and lost philosophical texts recovered from unreadable scrolls.
news_summary_fr: Les trésors culturels antiques ont bénéficié d'une nouvelle reconnaissance et d'une meilleure accessibilité grâce à l'inscription de Sardes au patrimoine mondial de l'UNESCO et à la redécouverte de textes philosophiques perdus, extraits de rouleaux devenus illisibles.
news_summary_es: Los tesoros culturales antiguos han adquirido un nuevo reconocimiento y se han hecho más accesibles gracias a que Sardis ha sido declarada Patrimonio de la Humanidad por la UNESCO y a la recuperación de textos filosóficos perdidos a partir de pergaminos ilegibles.

articles.title: After 70 years of excavation, ancient Sardis becomes a UNESCO World Heritage site
articles.summary: After nearly seven decades of excavation, the legendary ancient city of Sardis has become a UNESCO World Heritage Site, celebrating years of discoveries that continue to reshape its history. Archaeologists say the biggest breakthroughs don't happen in a single season—they emerge as decades of evidence slowly come together.
articles.content:
For thousands of years, the ancient city of Sardis in western Turkey changed hands as Greeks, Romans, Byzantines, and Ottomans rose and fell. Yet while the city's rulers changed repeatedly, one thing has remained remarkably steady. Since 1958, archaeologists have returned every year as part of the Harvard Cornell Exploration of Ancient Sardis, making it one of the world's longest running institutional excavation projects.

"It's really important that it has institutional continuity," said Benjamin Anderson, associate professor of history of art and visual studies in the College of Arts and Sciences. "Many of us know and have been mentored by colleagues of the previous generation of excavators. As a result, it's one of the few long-term archaeological projects in the region that has generated a critical mass of data."

For the past several years, Anderson has focused on documenting the walls and buildings of Sardis' acropolis, which became an important center during the Byzantine period after Roman rule.

"This is a city that shows up in lots of ancient historical sources," he said. "But now, just in the last 75 years or so, we have the possibility of telling that story, also, through what the project has found archaeologically."

This summer marked another milestone. Thanks to decades of excavation and the support of the local community, Sardis was added to the UNESCO World Heritage List.

"The opportunity to really start understanding a culture through the material remains is pretty unusual, and it requires that kind of long-term commitment," Anderson said. "That's also what's being celebrated by the World Heritage designation by UNESCO. This project has always been distinguished from the very beginning by a desire to communicate results and to make their work legible to tourists and to locals and all manner of different audiences."

Sardis Preserves Thousands of Years of History

Once the capital of the Iron Age kingdom of Lydia, Sardis occupied a strategic location between the Mediterranean and the Anatolian plateau. According to Annetta Alexandridis, associate professor of the history of art and classics in A&S, it served as "a place of cultural encounter between the East and West."

The Lydian era remains especially significant to archaeologists and historians. The Lydians are widely credited with inventing coinage, and their ruler, King Croesus, became legendary for his immense wealth. Alexander the Great later conquered Lydia, after which Sardis became part of the Roman Empire, followed by the Byzantine and Ottoman empires.

"Because it was not over built by a modern city -- it's only a little village -- Sardis gives you a really long history, from the Bronze Age, third millennium BCE, to basically today," Alexandridis said. "These layers are all there, and make it sometimes difficult to excavate, because they are not clearly stratified. They interfere with each other, but, in a way, it's an ongoing history, and that makes it so fascinating for us."

As associate director of the excavation, Alexandridis studies Roman funerary culture and is now leading a survey of Sardis' cemeteries, many of which have received far less attention than the nearby Bin Tepe cemetery, located about 10 kilometers north of the city. Bin Tepe contains some of the largest tumuli (burial mounds) ever recorded.

A Site That Shaped Archaeology

Sardis also occupies an important place in the history of American archaeology. The first modern excavation, led by the American Society for the Excavation of Sardis in the early 20th century, was "a really large-scale exploitation," Alexandridis said. Excavators uncovered the Temple of Artemis and the necropolis, but many artifacts were damaged, disappeared, or were taken to the United States through questionable means. Among them was a massive column that remains on display at the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

The project ended with the Greco-Turkish War in the early 1920s. Over the following decades, some artifacts gradually made their way back to Turkey.

"It's one of the first cases where we can see the whole discussion about restitution of antiquities that were illegally exported, until some were returned to Turkey," Alexandridis said. "It has all of these broader issues of how to deal with cultural heritage from a not only preservation or scholarly point of view, but also political and legal, and of the question of stewardship and responsibility for culture in the past."

The modern Harvard Cornell partnership began in 1958 under Harvard archaeologist George M. A. Hanfmann and Cornell architect Henry Detweiler from the College of Architecture, Art and Planning, whose expertise centered on documenting historic buildings.

"If you went to Sardis in 1950 there were a few things kind of sticking up above ground, but there was nothing really to see, per se," Anderson said. "The architects were the first generation of Cornellians who were there, and the project really committed to taking what they'd excavated responsibly, supplementing it through newly manufactured pieces, and presenting a total experience of the structure, instead of just producing a drawing and putting it in a publication."

During the 1950s and 1960s, the team reconstructed a monumental bath gymnasium complex and the largest synagogue in the ancient world. Those restoration efforts became influential models for similar work at archaeological sites elsewhere.

Since then, excavations have uncovered mud brick city walls, the acropolis, a Persian period garbage pit, a gold refining workshop, an ancient shopping district, and, most recently, a sanctuary plaza that required 15 years of excavation.

Training the Next Generation of Archaeologists

Today, the project is based at the Harvard Art Museums and includes researchers from Turkish institutions as well as several American universities, including the University of Wisconsin, Madison and the University of California, Berkeley. Cornell primarily contributes graduate students, along with an increasing number of undergraduates, who spend 10 weeks each summer working at the site.

Students either catalog recovered artifacts, most of which are ceramics, or "broken pots," as Anderson described them, or supervise excavation trenches.

Because Sardis sits on an alluvial plain, some trenches extend as much as 12 meters underground.

"[They're] quite terrifying in their own right," Anderson said.

"Local workers, who are already trained, gradually remove the soil, and the students are there observing, documenting, taking notes, asking questions, determining when they need to stop and call in maybe the director or an associate director to take a look at what's coming up, when they should take a photograph, when they should bring in the architects to make a state drawing of a particular moment," Anderson said.

According to Anderson, Sardis is one of only three excavation projects worldwide that "most people who go on to a career in classical archaeology in the U.S. have been through."

More than half of the researchers involved today are Turkish experts and students, and local participation remains central to the project's success.

"A topic that regularly accompanies what we are doing is how are we doing it? How do we include local expertise?" Alexandridis said.

Today, women from the Sardis region work alongside men in excavation and restoration efforts.

A Local Connection to an Ancient City

Leyla Uğurer, now a doctoral student in history of art and archaeology, grew up near Sardis. She first studied English language and literature at Istanbul University before deciding to pursue classical archaeology.

"To learn archaeology, you have to work at the site as well," she said.

Beginning in 2022, she surveyed rock cut tombs dating from the Lydian through Roman periods around Sardis. She continued that work for three summers before supervising the excavation of a late Roman site this year.

Her experience at Sardis inspired her to pursue a Ph.D. at Cornell, where Alexandridis became her adviser. Both share an interest in funerary art, which provides insights into beliefs about beauty, the afterlife, and everyday life.

This was the city on "one of the most important trade roads in the ancient world," where the first coin was minted and Alexander the Great visited, Uğurer said. "You were raised there, so you have the same culture going on in you and around you. I remember looking at archaeologists when I was a child and admiring them. To be familiar with those archaeological works going on also helps you understand the archaeological importance more."

She believes UNESCO recognition will bring important benefits to the region.

"As a local, I can say it is very important," she said. "First of all, now it is known worldwide and because of UNESCO, there can maybe be more funding for the excavation, also people, more tourists and more research. People will know the area much better, and there will be more protection."

Protecting Sardis for the Future

Greater protection is badly needed. Sardis' landscape is vulnerable to natural erosion, while many tumuli have already been damaged by farming. Looting has also become a serious problem.

Alexandridis said treasure hunters now operate on an "industrial dimensions" scale, using explosives, bulldozers, and often weapons to target ancient burial mounds.

Even after nearly seven decades of continuous excavation, researchers say Sardis still has much more to reveal.

"This is why the long-term commitment is so important," Anderson said. "One season's work, you'll learn how to do the thing, but you're not necessarily going to find something that will be especially significant for the history of the site, until maybe 10 years later, you find something else a little bit further away, and the pieces start to add up."
articles.reasoning: The article reports the significant achievement of the ancient city of Sardis becoming a UNESCO World Heritage Site after nearly 70 years of archaeological excavation. This designation reflects broad cultural and historical importance, promotes preservation, encourages tourism and research, and involves local community engagement. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights long-term scholarly and conservation efforts with meaningful positive impact on cultural heritage and education.
articles.personality_title: Ancient city of Sardis named UNESCO World Heritage site after 70 years of excavation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Sardis is an ancient city in western Turkey that has been important for thousands of years. It was once the capital of the kingdom of Lydia, known for creating the first coins. Over time, many empires ruled the city, including the Greeks, Romans, Byzantines, and Ottomans. Since 1958, archaeologists from Harvard and Cornell universities have been digging and studying Sardis every year.

**What happened** – After nearly 70 years of careful excavation and research, Sardis was officially added to the UNESCO World Heritage List. This means the city is now recognized worldwide for its cultural and historical value. The long-term project uncovered many important finds, such as ancient city walls, buildings, a gold workshop, and burial sites. The project also trains new archaeologists and involves local workers.

**Impact** – This UNESCO designation is special because it reflects many decades of steady work and cooperation between scientists and the local community. Sardis shows layers of history from the Bronze Age to today, making it a rare place to study thousands of years of human life. The site also helps teach new generations about archaeology and preserves the city from damage and looting.

**What's next step** – With UNESCO recognition, Sardis will receive more protection and funding. This can help archaeologists continue their work, improve the site for visitors, and support local people. Researchers believe there are still many discoveries waiting underground, so long-term study will continue.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After 70 years of excavation, Sardis’ new UNESCO status honors its rich history and helps protect this ancient city for future generations.
articles.personality_title_fr: La ville antique de Sardes devient site du patrimoine mondial de l'UNESCO après 70 ans de fouilles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Sardes est une ville antique située dans l'ouest de la Turquie, importante depuis des milliers d'années. Elle fut autrefois la capitale du royaume de Lydie, connu pour avoir créé les premières pièces de monnaie. Au fil du temps, de nombreux empires l'ont gouvernée, notamment les Grecs, Romains, Byzantins et Ottomans. Depuis 1958, des archéologues des universités Harvard et Cornell y travaillent chaque année.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Après près de 70 ans de fouilles et de recherches minutieuses, Sardes a été officiellement inscrite sur la liste du patrimoine mondial de l'UNESCO. Cela signifie que la ville est désormais reconnue mondialement pour sa valeur culturelle et historique. Le projet a permis de découvrir de nombreux éléments importants, comme des murs anciens, des bâtiments, un atelier d'or, et des sites funéraires. Le chantier forme aussi de nouveaux archéologues et fait appel aux habitants locaux.

**Impact** – Cette reconnaissance par l'UNESCO est unique car elle résulte de plusieurs décennies de travail régulier et de collaboration entre scientifiques et communauté locale. Sardes montre des couches d'histoire de l'âge du bronze jusqu'à aujourd'hui, ce qui en fait un lieu rare pour étudier des milliers d'années de vie humaine. Le site aide aussi à former les nouvelles générations et à protéger la ville contre les dégâts et le pillage.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec le statut UNESCO, Sardes bénéficiera de plus de protection et de financement. Cela permettra aux archéologues de poursuivre leurs recherches, d'améliorer le site pour les visiteurs, et de soutenir les habitants. Les chercheurs pensent qu'il reste encore beaucoup à découvrir sous terre, donc les études à long terme continueront.

**En une phrase** – Après 70 ans de fouilles, le nouveau statut UNESCO de Sardes honore son histoire riche et aide à protéger cette ville antique pour les générations futures.
articles.personality_title_es: La antigua ciudad de Sardis se convierte en Patrimonio Mundial de la UNESCO tras 70 años de excavaciones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Sardis es una ciudad antigua en el oeste de Turquía que ha sido importante durante miles de años. Fue la capital del reino de Lidia, conocido por crear las primeras monedas. Con el tiempo, muchos imperios gobernaron la ciudad, incluidos los griegos, romanos, bizantinos y otomanos. Desde 1958, arqueólogos de las universidades de Harvard y Cornell excavan y estudian Sardis cada año.

**Qué pasó** – Después de casi 70 años de excavaciones y estudios cuidadosos, Sardis fue incluida oficialmente en la Lista del Patrimonio Mundial de la UNESCO. Esto significa que la ciudad es reconocida mundialmente por su valor cultural e histórico. El proyecto descubrió muchos hallazgos importantes, como muros antiguos, edificios, un taller de oro y sitios funerarios. También forma a nuevos arqueólogos y trabaja con la comunidad local.

**Impacto** – Esta designación de la UNESCO es especial porque refleja décadas de trabajo constante y cooperación entre científicos y la comunidad local. Sardis muestra capas de historia desde la Edad del Bronce hasta hoy, lo que la convierte en un lugar raro para estudiar miles de años de vida humana. El sitio también ayuda a enseñar a nuevas generaciones y protege la ciudad del daño y el saqueo.

**Próximo paso** – Con el reconocimiento de la UNESCO, Sardis recibirá más protección y fondos. Esto ayudará a los arqueólogos a continuar su trabajo, mejorar el sitio para los visitantes y apoyar a la gente local. Los investigadores creen que aún hay muchos descubrimientos bajo tierra, por lo que el estudio a largo plazo continuará.

**Frase clave** – Tras 70 años de excavaciones, el nuevo estatus de Sardis como Patrimonio Mundial reconoce su rica historia y ayuda a proteger esta antigua ciudad para las futuras generaciones.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient layered archaeological site showing partially excavated ruins of mudbrick walls, a monumental bath complex, and a large synagogue foundation, with symbolic golden coins scattered gently across the ground and a silhouette of a mountain in the background representing Sardis’ enduring history; soft earth tones and muted blues highlight the connection between past civilizations and ongoing careful excavation beneath a calm sky.

articles.title: Lost books by ancient philosophers recovered from 'unreadable' scrolls
articles.summary: Scrolls from the Roman library of Herculaneum that were carbonised by a volcanic eruption have been read in their entirety for the first time, thanks to scans and AI software
articles.content:
Scrolls from the Roman library of Herculaneum that were carbonised by a volcanic eruption have been read in their entirety for the first time, thanks to scans and AI software

A carbonised scroll from Herculaneum that has now been read using advanced imaging techniques Paolo Verzone/National Geographic

Long-lost works of ancient philosophy have been recovered from papyrus scrolls that were scorched by the AD 79 eruption of Mount Vesuvius and thought to be impossible to read. For the first time, researchers have used AI to extract the entire surviving text from super-high-resolution 3D scans of a scroll without unrolling it.

The scrolls come from the library of Herculaneum, which was buried along with Pompeii nearly 2000 years ago. Scholars have been trying to read the carbonised scrolls, which resemble lumps of charcoal, since the library was discovered in 1752. Physically unwrapping them risks their destruction and the ink they are written in is mostly indistinguishable from the charred papyri – at least to human eyes.

Since 2023, however, the Vesuvius Challenge project has used particle accelerators to scan dozens of scrolls and provided the scans to an online community, who have helped write AI software to digitally unwrap the scrolls and detect ink on them. The approach has made book titles, authors and short passages readable.

Now, though, the team has uncovered 1.5 metres of text, written across 22 columns, from a 2-centimetre-wide scroll core whose outer layers were stripped off by scholars through the centuries in an effort to read it.

“We find records of several attempts to open it… but they couldn’t read anything,” says Federica Nicolardi at the University of Naples Federico II in Italy. “There are some fragments surviving from the last attempt to physically open it, but you can really see just a couple of letters. So virtual unwrapping was able to change the history of this papyrus.”

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

The scroll is what Vesuvius Challenge co-founder Brent Seales at the University of Kentucky refers to as an “impossible scroll”, one of hundreds that survived the assaults of early papyrologists, who could only read the “easier ones”.

Longer sections of the impossible scrolls are now becoming readable due to a combination of higher-resolution imaging, down to 2 micrometres, and more scan data for training the team’s data-hungry AI algorithms, he says.

Currently, their AI models are adapted to individual scrolls due to differences in, for example, the inks used. But Seales hopes that when the AI has seen enough of the collection, it will be capable of finding ink on any of them. “That’s where we are with large language models,” he says. “But that’s because they’ve trained those models repeatedly on the entire internet, and we’re not there yet with scrolls.”

The unwrapped text speaks of ethics, art and human nature, making multiple references to the Stoic doctrine. It is typical of scripts from the 2nd century BC, says Nicolardi, and it mentions the nephew of the Greek Stoic philosopher Chrysippus – making Chrysippus himself “the most natural candidate for authorship”, she says.

Chrysippus is regarded as one of the architects of Stoicism, but almost all his work was lost to history. According to classicist Thomas Coward at the University of Bristol, UK, we mainly know of it through other, often critical authors.

“To have access to a source text rather than quotes and summaries, which can be modified or interpreted by other writers, is very important,” he says, likening such a discovery to uncovering lost works by Isaac Newton or Albert Einstein that were only referenced by other scientists.

A text by the philosopher Philodemus from a Herculaneum scroll Vesuvius Challenge

One of Chrysippus’s critics was the lesser-known 1st-century Epicurean philosopher Philodemus, whose own works in the Herculaneum library were sponsored by its presumed owner: Julius Caesar’s father-in-law, Lucius Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus.

Another scroll made readable by the scans has been identified as On Gods, Book 8 by Philodemus, extending his previously known work On Gods, Book 1 to at least an eight-book series.

The findings represent extraordinary progress in imaging and the computational methods required for digital unwrapping, says Nicholas Freer at the University of Newcastle, UK, who believes the techniques could “radically transform” our understanding of ancient worlds.

“The reason why these developments matter so much is that hundreds of scrolls still remain unopened,” he says. “What we’re seeing now isn’t just a single, spectacular breakthrough. We’re witnessing the beginning of what could be a decades-long process of recovery.”

For Seales, who has pioneered digital unwrapping techniques for decades, the discoveries mark a transition from obsessing about whether the technology works to letting the scrolls do the talking.

“What people are going to care about now is: whose name actually appears, how old is the scroll and what does it say about philosophy?” he says. “So we’re working ourselves out of a job, but it’s all about restoring the lost voices.”

If he has any regrets, it is that so many scrolls were destroyed before he got a chance to read them. “The ones they pulled from the ground… the original 1752 scrolls, I believe we would be reading them instantly [now] because they were the easiest ones to read,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in recovering lost ancient philosophical texts using AI and advanced imaging, which has broad cultural and historical importance. This achievement opens the door to recovering hundreds of other scrolls, potentially transforming our understanding of ancient knowledge and philosophy. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful positive impact on human heritage.
articles.personality_title: Lost ancient scrolls read for the first time using AI and advanced scans
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nearly 2,000 years ago, a volcanic eruption buried the Roman town of Herculaneum and its library. The scrolls in this library were burnt and turned into blackened lumps, making them impossible to read by normal means.

**What happened** – Scientists have now used special 3D scans and artificial intelligence (AI) to read the texts inside these carbonised scrolls without unrolling them. For the first time, they recovered 1.5 meters of ancient writing from a tiny 2-centimeter scroll. This was done by scanning the scroll at very high resolution and using AI software to digitally unwrap and find the faded ink.

**Impact** – This breakthrough reveals lost works of ancient philosophy, including writings linked to Chrysippus, a major Stoic thinker whose original texts were mostly missing. Reading these scrolls gives historians direct access to ideas that were only known before through quotes by others. It also shows that hundreds of other scrolls might be opened using these methods, promising many more discoveries about the ancient world.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to improve their AI so it can read different scrolls more easily, even with different types of ink. As more scrolls are scanned and decoded, this technology could help uncover many more lost texts, changing what we know about history and philosophy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, AI and advanced imaging have unlocked ancient scrolls thought unreadable, revealing lost philosophical texts and opening the door to many more discoveries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des rouleaux anciens perdus lus pour la première fois grâce à l’IA et à des scans avancés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a près de 2 000 ans, une éruption volcanique a enseveli la ville romaine d’Herculanum et sa bibliothèque. Les rouleaux de cette bibliothèque ont été carbonisés, devenant des morceaux noirs difficiles à lire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé des scans 3D spéciaux et l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour lire ces rouleaux sans les dérouler. Pour la première fois, ils ont récupéré 1,5 mètre de texte ancien à partir d’un rouleau de seulement 2 centimètres. Ils ont scanné le rouleau avec une très haute résolution et utilisé l’IA pour déplier numériquement et détecter l’encre effacée.

**Impact** – Cette avancée révèle des œuvres perdues de philosophie ancienne, notamment des écrits liés à Chrysippe, un grand penseur stoïcien dont les textes originaux étaient presque disparus. Lire ces rouleaux donne aux historiens un accès direct à des idées connues auparavant seulement par des citations. Cela montre aussi que des centaines d’autres rouleaux pourraient être ouverts grâce à cette méthode, promettant de nombreuses découvertes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent améliorer l’IA pour qu’elle puisse lire plus facilement différents rouleaux, même avec des encres variées. En scannant et déchiffrant plus de rouleaux, cette technologie pourrait révéler de nombreux autres textes perdus, changeant notre compréhension de l’histoire et de la philosophie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, l’IA et des scans avancés ont déchiffré des rouleaux anciens jugés illisibles, révélant des textes philosophiques perdus et ouvrant la voie à de nombreuses autres découvertes.
articles.personality_title_es: Rolos antiguos perdidos leídos por primera vez gracias a la IA y escaneos avanzados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace casi 2,000 años, una erupción volcánica enterró la ciudad romana de Herculano y su biblioteca. Los rollos de esta biblioteca se carbonizaron, volviéndose pedazos negros difíciles de leer.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos usaron escaneos 3D especiales e inteligencia artificial (IA) para leer estos rollos sin desenrollarlos. Por primera vez, recuperaron 1.5 metros de texto antiguo de un rollo de solo 2 centímetros. Escanearon el rollo con muy alta resolución y usaron IA para desenrollarlo digitalmente y detectar la tinta borrada.

**Impacto** – Este avance revela obras perdidas de filosofía antigua, incluyendo escritos vinculados a Crisipo, un importante pensador estoico cuyos textos originales casi se habían perdido. Leer estos rollos da a los historiadores acceso directo a ideas que antes solo conocían por citas. También muestra que cientos de otros rollos podrían abrirse con estos métodos, prometiendo muchos más descubrimientos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren mejorar la IA para que pueda leer diferentes rollos más fácilmente, incluso con distintos tipos de tinta. A medida que más rollos se escaneen y descifren, esta tecnología podría ayudar a revelar muchos otros textos perdidos, cambiando lo que sabemos sobre la historia y la filosofía.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Por primera vez, la IA y la imagen avanzada han desbloqueado rollos antiguos considerados ilegibles, revelando textos filosóficos perdidos y abriendo la puerta a muchos más descubrimientos.
articles.image_prompt: An intricately detailed painting of a partially unrolled ancient papyrus scroll glowing softly with warm golden light, surrounded by delicate, translucent layers symbolizing digital 3D scans and AI algorithms gently peeling back the charred, blackened exterior to reveal elegant Greek script beneath, set against a muted, natural-toned background evoking the buried Roman library of Herculaneum.
